elements.sent-2411-002: Using $e^+e^-$ collision data corresponding to an integrated luminosity of 19\,fb$^{-1}$ collected by the BESIII detector at center-of-mass energies ranging from 4.13 to 4.70\,GeV, we report the first evidence for a new excited $\Omega^{-}$ hyperon, the $\Omega^*(2109)^{-}$, through the process $e^+ e^- \to \Omega^*(2109)^{- \bar{\Omega ^{+ +c.c.$ with a significance of 3.7\,$\sigma$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-003: The mass and width of $\Omega^*(2109)^{-}$ are measured to be $2108.8 \pm 5.5_{\rm stat \pm 1.5_{\rm syst}\,{\rm MeV /c^{2}$ and $21.6 \pm 17.7_{\rm stat \pm 9.4_{\rm syst}\,{\rm MeV}$, respectively.}}}
elements.sent-2411-004: We also present evidence for production of the $\Omega^*(2012)^{-}$ in the process $e^+ e^- \to \Omega^*(2012)^{- \bar{\Omega ^{+ +c.c.$ with a significance of 3.7\,$\sigma$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-005: Understanding their structure and spectroscopy is a necessary component of a real understanding of QCD.
elements.sent-2411-006: However, many fundamental issues in baryon spectroscopy are still not well understood~\cite{Capstick:2000dk, baryonspectrum .}
elements.sent-2411-007: One of the most important problems among them is the missing excited baryons: the number of observed baryon excited states is significantly smaller than the number predicted in the quark model~\cite{baryonspectrum, understandbaryon, diquark .}
elements.sent-2411-008: Thus, our present understanding of baryon spectroscopy is clearly incomplete.
elements.sent-2411-009: Compared with other light baryon spectra, our knowledge of the $\Omega^{-}$ hyperon spectrum~\cite{pdg2024 is still very sparse, motivating further searches for $\Omega^{-}$ hyperon excited states.}
elements.sent-2411-010: Before 2018, the Particle Data Group (PDG)~\cite{pdg2018 only listed three $\Omega^{-}$ resonances beyond the ground state $\Omega(1672)^{-}$, i.e. the $\Omega^*(2250)^-$, $\Omega^*(2380)^-$, and $\Omega^*(2470)^-$, and none of these three are considered well-established.}
elements.sent-2411-011: The Skyrme model~\cite{Oh:2007cr , the quark model~\cite{Capstick:1986ter, Faustov:2015eba, Chao:1980em, Chen:2009de, Pervin:2007wa, Liu:2019wdr , the models with hyperfine interactions between quarks~\cite{An:2013zoa, An:2014lga , and lattice QCD~\cite{Engel:2013ig, Edwards:2012fx, Bulava:2010HSC all predict a $J^P = 1/2^-$ and $J^P = 3/2^-$ pair of $\Omega^-$ excitations with masses lying between 1.7 and 2.2 GeV$/c^{2}$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-012: Searching for these missing resonant excitations is essential.
elements.sent-2411-013: In 2018, the Belle Collaboration reported the observation of the $\Omega^*(2012)^-$~\cite{omega2012 , which is a candidate excited $\Omega^{-}$ state decaying into $\Xi^0 K^-$ and $\Xi^- K^0_S$.}
elements.sent-2411-014: The calculations of the $\Omega^{-}$ mass spectrum in various models~\cite{Liu:2019wdr,Faustov:2015eba,Engel:2013ig,Chen:2009de,Pervin:2007wa,Oh:2007cr,Capstick:1986ter,Chao:1980em show that the $\Omega^*(2012)^-$ resonance may be a good candidate for the first orbital excitation of the $\Omega^{-}$ baryon.}
elements.sent-2411-015: There are also other interpretations for the $\Omega^*(2012)^-$, such as a hadronic molecule state~\cite{Hu:2022pae,Ikeno:2022jpe,Liu:2020yen,Ikeno:2020vqv,Lin:2019tex,Gutsche:2019eoh,Zeng:2020och,Lu:2020ste,Pavao:2018xub,Valderrama:2018bmv,Lin:2018nqd, Huang:2018wth .}
elements.sent-2411-016: In 2019, the Belle Collaboration reported an upper limit of 0.12 for the ratio of the branching ratios of $\Omega^*(2012)^- \to \Xi \pi \bar K$ to $\Omega^*(2012)^- \to \Xi \bar K$~\cite{Belle:2019zco , which supports the conventional interpretation.}
elements.sent-2411-017: A later study from the Belle Collaboration reported an updated value of the ratio, i.e. $0.99 \pm 0.26 \pm 0.06$, based on the same data sample, but with different event selection criteria and signal parameterization~\cite{Belle:2022mrg , which favors the molecular interpretation of the $\Omega^*(2012)^-$.}
elements.sent-2411-018: The nature of the $\Omega^*(2012)^-$ is still controversial.
elements.sent-2411-019: Searching for the $\Omega^*(2012)^-$ and other new $\Omega^{-}$ excitations in different experiments, such as BESIII, will aid in understanding the nature of the $\Omega^*(2012)^-$ and other related states.
elements.sent-2411-020: In this Letter, we search for $\Omega^{*-}$ through the process $e^+ e^- \to \Omega^{*- \bar{\Omega ^{+ +c.c.$, using $e^+e^-$ collision data corresponding to a total integrated luminosity of 19\,fb$^{-1}$, collected by the BESIII detector at center-of-mass energies ranging from 4.13 to 4.70\,GeV~\cite{dataset .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-021: Our findings present the first evidence for a new excited $\Omega^-$ hyperon, designated as $\Omega^*(2109)^{-}$, along with evidence for a new production mechanism for the previously identified $\Omega^*(2012)^-$.
elements.sent-2411-022: Details about the design and performance of the BESIII detector are given in Refs.~\cite{Ablikim:2009aa,Ablikim:2019hff .}
elements.sent-2411-023: Simulated data samples produced with a {\sc geant4 -based~\cite{geant4 Monte Carlo (MC) package, which includes the geometric description of the BESIII detector and the detector response, are used to determine detection efficiencies and to estimate backgrounds.}}
elements.sent-2411-024: The simulation models the beam energy spread and initial-state radiation (ISR) in the $e^+e^-$ annihilations with the generator {\sc kkmc ~\cite{ref:kkmc .}}
elements.sent-2411-025: The inclusive MC sample includes the production of open charm processes, the ISR production of vector charmonium(-like) states, and the continuum processes incorporated in {\sc kkmc ~\cite{ref:kkmc .}}
elements.sent-2411-026: All particle decays are modelled with {\sc evtgen ~\cite{ref:evtgen using branching fractions either taken from the PDG~\cite{pdg2024 , when available, or otherwise estimated with {\sc lundcharm ~\cite{ref:lundcharm .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-027: Final state radiation from charged final state particles is incorporated using the {\sc photos package~\cite{photos2 .}}
elements.sent-2411-028: We search for potential excited $\Omega^-$ states in the recoil mass spectrum of the $\bar{\Omega ^+$ in the $e^+ e^- \to \Omega^{*- \bar{\Omega ^{+}$ process, with $\bar{\Omega ^+ \to \bar{\Lambda K^+$ and $\bar{\Lambda \to \bar{p \pi^+$.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-029: Unless explicitly stated, charge-conjugate modes are always implied throughout this Letter.
elements.sent-2411-030: Charged tracks detected in the multilayer drift chamber (MDC) are required to be within a polar angle ($\theta$) range of $|\rm{cos\theta |<0.93$, where $\theta$ is defined with respect to the $z$-axis, which is the symmetry axis of the MDC.}
elements.sent-2411-031: Particle identification for charged tracks combines measurements of the energy deposited in the MDC and the flight time in the time-of-flight system to form likelihoods $\mathcal{L (h)~(h=p,K,\pi)$ for each hadron $h$ hypothesis.}
elements.sent-2411-032: Each track is assigned to the particle type with the highest $\mathcal{L (h)$.}
elements.sent-2411-033: Events with at least one $\bar{p}$, one $K^+$, and one $\pi^+$ are kept for further analysis.
elements.sent-2411-034: The $\bar{\Lambda}$ ($\bar{\Omega ^+$) candidates are reconstructed from $\bar{p}\pi^+$ ($\bar{\Lambda K^+$) pairs which are constrained to originate from a common vertex by requiring the $\chi^2$ of the vertex fits to be less than 50, and the reconstructed invariant masses are required to be within $0.005~(0.010)\,GeV/c^{2}$ of the nominal $m_{\bar{\Lambda }$ ($m_{\bar{\Omega ^{+ }$) masses~\cite{pdg2024 .}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-035: The distance between the decay vertices of the $\bar{\Lambda}$ ($\bar{\Omega ^+$) candidates and the $e^+e^-$ interaction point is required to be greater than twice the fitted uncertainties.}
elements.sent-2411-036: If there are multiple combinations, all $\bar{\Lambda}$ candidates are kept, while among the $\bar{\Omega ^+$ candidates, the one with the minimum vertex fit $\chi^2$ is retained for further analysis.}
elements.sent-2411-037: The 2D-distribution of reconstructed mass of the $\bar{\Lambda}$ candidate, $M_{\bar{\Lambda }$, vs.~the mass of the $\bar{\Omega ^+$ candidate, $M_{\bar{\Omega ^{+ }$, is shown in Fig.~\ref{fig_2d_lmd_omega for data summed over all energy points.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-038: Clear signals of the $\bar{\Lambda}$ and the $\bar{\Omega ^+$ can be seen.}
elements.sent-2411-039: The sideband regions are chosen as $|M_{\bar{\Omega ^{+ -m_{\bar{\Omega ^{+ | \in [0.02, 0.06]\,{\rm GeV /c^{2}$ and $|M_{\bar{\Lambda -m_{\bar{\Lambda |< 0.005\,{\rm GeV /c^{2}$.}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=1\linewidth]{fig1_2D_mass_lmd_omega.eps} \caption{ Distribution of $M_{\bar{\Lambda}}$ versus $M_{\bar{\Omega}^{+}}$ for data. The blue box represents the signal region, and the red boxes indicate the sideband regions. } \label{fig_2d_lmd_omega}
elements.sent-2411-040: Figure~\ref{fig_recoil_mass_signal_region shows the $\bar{\Omega ^+$ recoil-mass spectrum for the candidate events falling into the signal region in Fig.~\ref{fig_2d_lmd_omega , calculated using $RM_{\bar{\Omega ^+ +M_{\bar{\Omega ^+ -m_{\bar{\Omega ^+}$.}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-041: Here, $RM_{\bar{\Omega ^+ =|p_{e^+e^- -p_{\bar{\Omega ^+ |$, where $p_{e^+e^-}$ is the four-momentum of the initial $e^+e^-$ system and $p_{\bar{\Omega ^+}$ is the four-momentum of the $\bar{\Omega ^+$.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-042: The variable $RM_{\bar{\Omega ^+ +M_{\bar{\Omega ^+ -m_{\bar{\Omega ^+}$ provides better resolution compared to $RM_{\bar{\Omega ^+}$~\cite{zcs .}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-043: A clear $\Omega^{-}$ ground state signal near $1.67\,{\rm GeV /c^{2}$ is seen, plus two additional peaks near $2.0\,{\rm GeV /c^{2}$ and $2.1\,{\rm GeV /c^{2}$, which we refer to as the $\Omega^*(2012)^{-}$ and $\Omega^*(2109)^{-}$, respectively.}}}
elements.sent-2411-044: Overall, except for these three peaks, the data sideband shape describes the data well.
elements.sent-2411-045: Since the background distribution is smooth in MC studies, the data sideband events are used to constrain the shape of the background in the subsequent fit.
elements.figure-2411-002: \subfigure{\includegraphics[width=1.0\linewidth]{fig2_signal_fit_mass_tag_reco.eps} \label{fig_recoil_mass_signal_region} } \subfigure{\includegraphics[width=1.0\linewidth]{fig2_sideband_fit_mass_tag_reco.eps} \label{fig_recoil_mass_sideband_region} } \caption{ Simultaneous maximum likelihood fit the $RM_{\bar{\Omega}^+}+M_{\bar{\Omega}^+}-m_{\bar{\Omega}^+}$ distributions from the signal (a) and sideband (b) regions. The black dots with error bars represent data. The blue solid curve is the total fit result. The red long dashed, red short dashed, and red double-dot-dashed lines are the signal shapes for $\Omega(1672)^{-}$, $\Omega^*(2012)^{-}$, and $\Omega^*(2109)^{-}$, respectively. The black dot-dashed line is the background shape. } \label{fig_recoil_mass}
elements.sent-2411-046: A simultaneous maximum likelihood fit is performed to the $\bar{\Omega ^+$ recoil-mass spectra for the candidate events from the signal (Fig.~\ref{fig_recoil_mass a) and sideband (Fig.~\ref{fig_recoil_mass b) regions.}}}
elements.sent-2411-047: In this fit, the signal shapes for the $\Omega^*(2012)^{-}$ and $\Omega^*(2109)^{-}$ are Breit-Wigner functions convolved with a double-Gaussian resolution function; the $\Omega(1672)^{-}$ is described by an asymmetric double-Gaussian function, and the background is parameterized by a third-order Chebyshev polynomial.
elements.sent-2411-048: The parameters of the Breit-Wigner function for $\Omega^*(2012)^{-}$ are fixed to the PDG values~\cite{pdg2024 , the parameters of the double-Gaussian resolution functions are fixed according to the MC simulation, and all other parameters for the signal shapes are free.}
elements.sent-2411-049: The parameters of the third-order Chebyshev polynomial are free and shared for both of data samples from the signal and sideband regions.
elements.sent-2411-050: The statistical significances of the two $\Omega^{-}$ excitations are calculated by excluding the corresponding peak from the fit, finding the change in the log-likelihood $\Delta({\rm 2ln}\mathcal{L )$, and converting this to a $p$-value, taking into account the change in degrees of freedom ($N_{\rm{dof }$)~\cite{Wilks:1938dza .}}}
elements.sent-2411-051: This is then converted to an effective number of standard deviations $n_{\sigma}$.
elements.sent-2411-052: For the peak around $2.0\,{\rm GeV /c^{2}$, we obtain $\Delta({\rm 2ln}\mathcal{L ) = 13.8$ with $N_{\rm{dof = 1$, yielding $n_{\sigma =3.7$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-053: This provides evidence for $\Omega^*(2012)^{-}$ production, with a signal yield of $28 \pm 9$.
elements.sent-2411-054: For the peak around $2.1\,{\rm GeV /c^{2}$, we obtain $\Delta({\rm 2ln}\mathcal{L ) = 20.8$ with $N_{\rm{dof = 3$, yielding $n_{\sigma =3.9$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-055: This gives evidence for a new excited $\Omega^{-}$ hyperon, the $\Omega^*(2109)^{-}$, with signal yield of $52 \pm 20$.
elements.sent-2411-056: The fitted mass and width of the $\Omega^*(2109)^{-}$ are $m_{\Omega^*(2109)^{- =2108.8 \pm 5.5_{\rm stat}\,{\rm MeV /c^{2}$ and ${\Gamma _{\Omega^*(2109)^{- =21.6 \pm 17.7_{\rm stat}\,{\rm MeV .$ Systematic uncertainties on the measurements of the mass and width for the $\Omega^*(2109)^{-}$ mainly originate from the background shape, the mass shift, the resolution, the efficiency curve, the choice of sideband, and the parametrization of $\Omega^*(2012)^{-}$ signal shape.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-057: The numerical results are summarized in Table~\ref{tab_sys .}
elements.sent-2411-058: The impact of the background shape is evaluated by varying it to be a fourth-order Chebyshev polynomial.
elements.sent-2411-059: The uncertainties from the mass shift and resolution are determined by calibrating the mass shift and resolution difference between data and MC simulation using the control sample of $e^+ e^- \to \Omega^{- \bar{\Omega ^{+}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-060: The effects of the dependence of efficiency on the fitted variable are assigned according to the differences between the results obtained with and without consideration of the efficiency dependence.
elements.sent-2411-061: The systematic uncertainty related to the choice of sideband is estimated by ten different variations in the edges of the sideband intervals, by amounts ranging from 5-20\,MeV/$c^2$, and the maximum change of the mass or width compared to the nominal one is taken as the uncertainty.
elements.sent-2411-062: To consider the uncertainty from the parametrization of $\Omega^*(2012)^{-}$ signal shape, we change the fixed parameters of $\Omega^*(2012)^{-}$ adopted from PDG~\cite{pdg2024 by $\pm 1\sigma$, and the largest variation of the mass or width is considered as one contribution to this uncertainty; meanwhile, the variation of the mass and width when the $\Omega^*(2012)^{-}$ is alternatively fitted by the signal MC shape taking into account the shape distortion of $\Omega^*(2012)^{-}$ due to the finite phase space in $\Omega^*(2012)^{-}$ three-body decays (such as $\Omega^*(2012)^{- \to \Xi^*(1530) K \to \Xi \pi K$) is considered as another contribution to this uncertainty.}}
elements.sent-2411-063: The potential contribution from the feed-down component ($e^+e^- \to \bar{\Omega ^+ \Omega^*(2109)^-, \Omega^*(2109)^- \to \Omega^- \pi \pi$) is investigated, and no evidence of this contribution is found in current statistics.}
elements.figure-2411-003: \setlength{\tabcolsep}{1mm} \caption{Absolute systematic uncertainties for the measurements of the resonance parameters of $\Omega^*(2109)^{-}$. } \renewcommand{\arraystretch}{1.0} \begin{tabular}{ccc} \specialrule{0em}{1pt}{1pt} \multirow{2}{*}{Source} Mass Width (${\rm MeV}/c^{2}$) ($\rm MeV$) Background shape 1.4 7.9 Mass shift 0.3 ··· Resolution ··· 4.7 Efficiency curve 0.2 0.3 Choice of sideband 0.4 1.8 Parametrization of $\Omega^*(2012)^{-}$ \multirow{2}{*}{0.1} \multirow{2}{*}{0.2} signal shape Total 1.5 9.4 \end{tabular} \label{tab_sys}
elements.sent-2411-064: Following Ref.~\cite{zcs , we take the look-elsewhere effect and the relevant systematic uncertainties into account simultaneously to re-estimate the significance of the $\Omega^*(2109)^{-}$ using 5000 pseudodatasets to obtain the distribution of $\Delta({\rm 2ln}\mathcal{L )$.}}
elements.sent-2411-065: The resulting distribution is found to be well described by a $\chi^2$ distribution with $3.27 \pm 0.03$ degrees of freedom.
elements.sent-2411-066: Combining with the minimum $\Delta({\rm 2ln}\mathcal{L )=20.6$ among all the systematic tests, the significance of the $\Omega^*(2109)^{-}$ is determined to be $n_{\sigma =3.7$ conservatively with $N_{\rm{dof = 3.27 + 0.03 = 3.30$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-067: For the significance of the $\Omega^*(2012)^{-}$, to account for the systematic effects, the minimum $\Delta({\rm 2ln}\mathcal{L )$ among all systematic variations is taken, which is still 6.9.}
elements.sent-2411-068: Combining with $N_{\rm{dof = 1$, we obtain $n_{\sigma =3.7$ for $\Omega^*(2012)^{-}$ including systematic uncertainties.}}}
elements.sent-2411-069: Since both of the significances of $\Omega^*(2012)^{-}$ and $\Omega^*(2109)^{-}$ are in the range of [3, 5]\,$\sigma$, we report the ratios of the average cross sections of $e^+ e^- \to \Omega^*(X)^{- \bar{\Omega ^{+ +c.c.$ to $e^+ e^- \to \Omega^{- \bar{\Omega ^{+}$, $R_{\Omega^*(X)^{- }$, in the range of $\sqrt{s \in [4.13, 4.70]\,GeV$ and also the corresponding upper limits at the 90\% confidence level, $R_{\Omega^*(X)^{- ^{\rm UL}$.}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-070: Here, $R_{\Omega^*(X)^{- \equiv \frac{\sigma(e^+ e^- \to \Omega^*(X)^{- \bar{\Omega ^{+ +c.c.) {\sigma(e^+ e^- \to \Omega^{- \bar{\Omega ^{+ )}$, with $\Omega^*(X)^{-}$ denoting $\Omega^*(2012)^{-}$ or $\Omega^*(2109)^{-}$, and are determined as \begin{equation}R_{\Omega^*(X)^{-}} \,=\, \frac{N_{\Omega^*(X)^{-}}}{N_{\Omega^{-}}} \left( \frac{(1+\delta^r)_{\Omega^{-}}}{(1+\delta^r)_{\Omega^*(X)^{-}}} \right) \left( \frac{\epsilon_{\Omega^{-}}}{\epsilon_{\Omega^*(X)^{-}}} \right) , \label{eq_cs_2109_all}\end{equation} where $N_{\Omega^*(X)^{- }$ and $N_{\Omega^{- }$ represent the numbers of events, $(1+\delta^r)_{\Omega^*(X)^{- }$ and $(1+\delta^r)_{\Omega^{- }$ represent the ISR correction factors, and $\epsilon_{\Omega^*(X)^{- }$ and $\epsilon_{\Omega^{- }$ represent the detection efficiencies for $e^+ e^- \to \Omega^*(X)^{- \bar{\Omega ^{+}$ and $e^+ e^- \to \Omega^{- \bar{\Omega ^{+}$, respectively.}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-071: Based on these values and Eq.~(\ref{eq_cs_2109_all ), we obtain $R_{\Omega^*(2012)^{- =0.42\pm0.15_{\rm stat}$ and $R_{\Omega^*(2109)^{- =0.92\pm0.37_{\rm stat}$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-072: The systematic uncertainties for $\frac{N_{\Omega^*(X)^{- {N_{\Omega^{- }$ come from sources similar to those for the measurements of the $\Omega^*(2109)^{-}$ parameters, including the background shape, the resolution, the efficiency curve, the choice of sideband, and the parametrization of $\Omega^*(2012)^{-}$ signal shape.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-073: Thus, the same variation methods are adopted to estimate the corresponding effects here.
elements.sent-2411-074: Following the same method used in Ref.~\cite{BESIII:2024ext , the systematic uncertainties for $\frac{(1+\delta^r)_{\Omega^{- {(1+\delta^r)_{\Omega^*(X)^{- }$ are assigned by changing the input cross section line shape to the phase pace line shape.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-075: The systematic uncertainties associated with $\frac{\epsilon_{\Omega^{- {\epsilon_{\Omega^*(X)^{- }$ are examined from two perspectives: (1) the differences in $\frac{\epsilon_{\Omega^{- {\epsilon_{\Omega^*(X)^{- }$ obtained from two different MC samples, with the recoil $\Omega^*(X)^{-}$, $\Omega^-$ decaying invisibly or exclusively to $\Xi K/\Lambda K$; (2) the variations in $\frac{\epsilon_{\Omega^{- {\epsilon_{\Omega^*(X)^{- }$ by changing the $\bar{\Omega ^{+}$ production angular model in the MC simulation to align with the expected distribution in a Generalized-Vector-Meson Dominance Model~\cite{Korner:1986vi , assuming the $J^P$ for $\Omega^*(2102)^-$ and $\Omega^*(2109)^-$ are $1/2^-$ and $3/2^-$, respectively.}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-076: The corresponding numerical values are summarized in Table~\ref{tab_sys_R .}
elements.sent-2411-077: Based on a Bayesian method~\cite{bayesian , the raw likelihood distributions versus the corresponding $R_{\Omega^*(X)^{- }$ are obtained.}}
elements.sent-2411-078: Then, the likelihood distribution after considering the additive systematic uncertainty is smeared with a Gaussian function with the mean of zero and the width of the total multiplicative systematic uncertainty.
elements.sent-2411-079: Based on this, $R_{\Omega^*(2012)^{- ^{\rm UL}$ and $R_{\Omega^*(2109)^{- ^{\rm UL}$ are set to be 0.66 and 1.45, respectively.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-080: In summary, using data samples with an integrated luminosity of 19\,fb$^{-1}$ collected by the BESIII detector at center-of-mass energies ranging from 4.13 to 4.70\,GeV, we find an evidence for the $\Omega^*(2109)^{-}$ hyperon through the process $e^+ e^- \to \Omega^*(2109)^{- \bar{\Omega ^{+}$ with a significance of 3.7\,$\sigma$ including both the look-elsewhere effect and systematic uncertainties.}}
elements.sent-2411-081: We also find evidence for $\Omega^*(2012)^{-}$ production via the process $e^+ e^- \to \Omega^*(2012)^{- \bar{\Omega ^{+}$, with a significance of 3.7\,$\sigma$ including systematic uncertainties.}}
elements.sent-2411-082: Furthermore, the ratios $R_{\Omega^*(X)^{- }$ of the average cross sections of $e^+ e^- \to \Omega^*(X)^{- \bar{\Omega ^{+ +c.c.$ and $e^+ e^- \to \Omega^{- \bar{\Omega ^{+}$ in the range of $\sqrt{s \in [4.13, 4.70]\,GeV$ are measured as $R_{\Omega^*(2012)^{- =0.42\pm0.15_{\rm stat}\pm0.05_{\rm syst}$ and $R_{\Omega^*(2109)^{- =0.92\pm0.37_{\rm stat}\pm0.24_{\rm syst}$, while the corresponding upper limits at the 90\% confidence level are set at $R_{\Omega^*(2012)^{- ^{\rm UL =0.66$ and $R_{\Omega^*(2109)^{- ^{\rm UL =1.45$.}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-083: Evidence for the $\Omega^*(2109)^{-}$ and the $\Omega^*(2012)^{-}$ reported in this Letter helps to fill in the missing excited states in the $\Omega^-$ hyperon spectrum.
elements.sent-2411-084: Theoretical models~\cite{Oh:2007cr, Capstick:1986ter, Faustov:2015eba, Chao:1980em, Chen:2009de, Pervin:2007wa, Liu:2019wdr, An:2013zoa, An:2014lga, Engel:2013ig, Edwards:2012fx, Bulava:2010HSC predict a $J^P = 1/2^-$ and $J^P = 3/2^-$ pair of $\Omega^-$ excitations within the mass region from 1.7 to 2.2 GeV$/c^{2}$, but with large variations in their mass predictions.}
elements.sent-2411-085: We note that our results are very close to the lattice QCD calculation reported by the Hadron Spectrum Collaboration~\cite{Edwards:2012fx where the masses of the $J^P = 1/2^-$ and $J^P = 3/2^-$ pair of $\Omega^-$ excitations are predicted to be around 2.0 GeV$/c^{2}$ and 2.1 GeV$/c^{2}$, respectively.}
elements.sent-2411-086: This consistency would imply that the $\Omega^*(2109)^{-}$ and $\Omega^*(2012)^{-}$ are probably conventional three-quark baryons and not molecules or other exotic states.
elements.sent-2411-087: The BEPCII upgrade, being implemented in 2024, will lead to much higher luminosity, allowing the confirmation of and parameter measurements for these two states to be investigated with larger data samples.
elements.sent-2411-088: This work is supported in part by National Key R\&D Program of China under Contracts Nos.

elements.sent-2411-090: B16F640076, B50G670107; Polish National Science Centre under Contract No. 2019/35/O/ST2/02907; Swedish Research Council under Contract No. 2019.04595; The Swedish Foundation for International Cooperation in Research and Higher Education under Contract No. CH2018-7756; U.. S.
elements.sent-2411-091: Department of Energy under Contract No. DE-FG02-05ER41374.
elements.sent-2411-092: Using $e^+e^-$ collision data corresponding to an integrated luminosity of 19\,fb$^{-1}$ collected by the BESIII detector at center-of-mass energies ranging from 4.13 to 4.70\,GeV, we report the first evidence for a new excited $\Omega^{-}$ hyperon, the $\Omega(2109)^{-}$, through the process $e^+ e^- \to \Omega(2109)^{- \bar{\Omega ^{+ +c.c.$ with a significance of 4.1\,$\sigma$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-093: The mass and width of $\Omega(2109)^{-}$ are measured to be $2108.5 \pm 5.2_{\rm stat \pm 0.9_{\rm syst}\,{\rm MeV /c^{2}$ and $18.3 \pm 16.4_{\rm stat \pm 5.7_{\rm syst}\,{\rm MeV}$, respectively.}}}
elements.sent-2411-094: We also present evidence for a new production mechanism for the previously identified $\Omega(2012)^-$ via the process $e^+ e^- \to \Omega(2012)^{- \bar{\Omega ^{+ +c.c.$ with a significance of 3.5\,$\sigma$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-095: Before 2018, the Particle Data Group (PDG)~\cite{pdg2018 only listed three $\Omega^{-}$ resonances beyond the ground state $\Omega^{-}$, i.e. the $\Omega(2250)^-$, $\Omega(2380)^-$, and $\Omega(2470)^-$, and none of these three are considered well-established.}
elements.sent-2411-096: In 2018, the Belle Collaboration reported the observation of the $\Omega(2012)^-$~\cite{omega2012 , which is a candidate excited $\Omega^{-}$ state decaying into $\Xi^0 K^-$ and $\Xi^- K^0_S$.}
elements.sent-2411-097: The calculations of the $\Omega^{-}$ mass spectrum in various models~\cite{Liu:2019wdr,Faustov:2015eba,Engel:2013ig,Chen:2009de,Pervin:2007wa,Oh:2007cr,Capstick:1986ter,Chao:1980em show that the $\Omega(2012)^-$ resonance may be a good candidate for the first orbital excitation of the $\Omega^{-}$ baryon.}
elements.sent-2411-098: There are also other interpretations for the $\Omega(2012)^-$, such as a hadronic molecule state~\cite{Hu:2022pae,Ikeno:2022jpe,Liu:2020yen,Ikeno:2020vqv,Lin:2019tex,Gutsche:2019eoh,Zeng:2020och,Lu:2020ste,Pavao:2018xub,Valderrama:2018bmv,Lin:2018nqd, Huang:2018wth .}
elements.sent-2411-099: In 2019, the Belle Collaboration reported an upper limit of 0.12 for the ratio of the branching ratios of $\Omega(2012)^- \to \Xi \pi \bar K$ to $\Omega(2012)^- \to \Xi \bar K$~\cite{Belle:2019zco , which supports the conventional interpretation.}
elements.sent-2411-100: A later study from the Belle Collaboration reported an updated value of the ratio, i.e. $0.99 \pm 0.26 \pm 0.06$, based on the same data sample, but with different event selection criteria and signal parameterization~\cite{Belle:2022mrg , which favors the molecular interpretation of the $\Omega(2012)^-$.}
elements.sent-2411-101: The nature of the $\Omega(2012)^-$ is still controversial.
elements.sent-2411-102: Searching for the $\Omega(2012)^-$ and other new $\Omega^{-}$ excitations in different experiments, such as BESIII, will aid in understanding the nature of the $\Omega(2012)^-$ and other related states.
elements.sent-2411-103: Our findings present the first evidence for a new excited $\Omega^-$ hyperon, designated as $\Omega(2109)^{-}$, along with evidence for a new production mechanism for the previously identified $\Omega(2012)^-$.
elements.sent-2411-104: The $\bar{\Lambda}$ ($\bar{\Omega ^+$) candidates are reconstructed from $\bar{p}\pi^+$ ($\bar{\Lambda K^+$) pairs which are constrained to originate from a common vertex by requiring the $\chi^2$ of the vertex fits to be less than 50.}}
elements.sent-2411-105: The reconstructed $\bar{\Lambda}$ invariant mass, $M_{\bar{\Lambda }$, is required to be within $0.005\,GeV/c^{2}$ of the nominal $m_{\bar{\Lambda }$ mass~\cite{pdg2024 , and the $\bar{\Omega ^+$ invariant mass, calculated using $M_{\bar{\Omega ^{+ -M_{\bar{\Lambda +m_{\bar{\Lambda }$, is required to be within $0.010\,GeV/c^{2}$ of the nominal $m_{\bar{\Omega }$ mass~\cite{pdg2024 , where $M_{\bar{\Omega ^{+ }$ is the reconstructed $\bar{\Omega ^+$ mass.}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-106: The 2D-distribution of reconstructed mass of the $\bar{\Lambda}$ candidate, $M_{\bar{\Lambda }$, vs.~the invariant mass of the $\bar{\Omega ^+$ candidate, $M_{\bar{\Omega ^{+ -M_{\bar{\Lambda +m_{\bar{\Lambda }$, is shown in Fig.~\ref{fig_2d_lmd_omega for data summed over all energy points.}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-107: The sideband regions are chosen as $|M_{\bar{\Omega ^{+ -M_{\bar{\Lambda +m_{\bar{\Lambda -m_{\bar{\Omega ^{+ | \in [0.02, 0.06]\,{\rm GeV /c^{2}$ and $|M_{\bar{\Lambda -m_{\bar{\Lambda |< 0.005\,{\rm GeV /c^{2}$.}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.figure-2411-005: \includegraphics[width=1\linewidth]{fig1_2D_mass_lmd_omega.eps} \caption{ Distribution of $M_{\bar{\Lambda}}$ versus $M_{\bar{\Omega}^{+}}-M_{\bar{\Lambda}}+m_{\bar{\Lambda}}$ for data. The blue box represents the signal region, and the red boxes indicate the sideband regions. } \label{fig_2d_lmd_omega}
elements.sent-2411-108: A clear $\Omega^{-}$ ground state signal near $1.67\,{\rm GeV /c^{2}$ is seen, plus two additional peaks near $2.0\,{\rm GeV /c^{2}$ and $2.1\,{\rm GeV /c^{2}$, which we refer to as the $\Omega(2012)^{-}$ and $\Omega(2109)^{-}$, respectively.}}}
elements.sent-2411-109: Since the background is almost all from combinatorial sources and the background distribution is smooth in MC studies, the data sideband events are used to constrain the shape of the background in the subsequent fit.
elements.figure-2411-006: \subfigure{\includegraphics[width=1.0\linewidth]{fig2_signal_fit_mass_tag_reco.eps} \label{fig_recoil_mass_signal_region} } \subfigure{\includegraphics[width=1.0\linewidth]{fig2_sideband_fit_mass_tag_reco.eps} \label{fig_recoil_mass_sideband_region} } \caption{ Simultaneous unbinned maximum likelihood fit the $RM_{\bar{\Omega}^+}+M_{\bar{\Omega}^+}-m_{\bar{\Omega}^+}$ distributions from the signal (a) and sideband (b) regions. The black dots with error bars represent data. The blue solid curve is the total fit result. The red long dashed, red short dashed, and red double-dot-dashed lines are the signal shapes for $\Omega^{-}$, $\Omega(2012)^{-}$, and $\Omega(2109)^{-}$, respectively. The black dot-dashed line is the background shape. } \label{fig_recoil_mass}
elements.sent-2411-110: A simultaneous unbinned maximum likelihood fit is performed to the $\bar{\Omega ^+$ recoil-mass spectra for the candidate events from the signal (Fig.~\ref{fig_recoil_mass_signal_region ) and sideband (Fig.~\ref{fig_recoil_mass_sideband_region ) regions.}}}
elements.sent-2411-111: In this fit, the signal shapes for the $\Omega(2012)^{-}$ and $\Omega(2109)^{-}$ are non-relativistic Breit-Wigner functions convolved with a double-Gaussian resolution function; the $\Omega^{-}$ is described by an asymmetric double-Gaussian function, and the background is parameterized by an exponential function.
elements.sent-2411-112: The parameters of the Breit-Wigner function for $\Omega(2012)^{-}$ are fixed to the PDG values~\cite{pdg2024 , the parameters of the double-Gaussian resolution functions are fixed according to the MC simulation, and all other parameters for the signal shapes are free.}
elements.sent-2411-113: According to the MC simulation, the mass resolutions for the $\Omega^-$, $\Omega(2012)^-$, and $\Omega(2109)^-$ are about 13, 10, and 9 ${\rm MeV /c^{2}$, respectively.}
elements.sent-2411-114: The parameter of the exponential function is free and shared for both of data samples from the signal and sideband regions.
elements.sent-2411-115: For the peak around $2.0\,{\rm GeV /c^{2}$, we obtain $\Delta({\rm 2ln}\mathcal{L ) = 12.2$ with $N_{\rm{dof = 1$, yielding $n_{\sigma =3.5$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-116: This provides evidence for $\Omega(2012)^{-}$ production, with a signal yield of $24 \pm 8$.
elements.sent-2411-117: For the peak around $2.1\,{\rm GeV /c^{2}$, we obtain $\Delta({\rm 2ln}\mathcal{L ) = 23.8$ with $N_{\rm{dof = 3$, yielding $n_{\sigma =4.2$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-118: This gives evidence for a new excited $\Omega^{-}$ hyperon, the $\Omega(2109)^{-}$, with signal yield of $53 \pm 19$.
elements.sent-2411-119: The fitted mass and width of the $\Omega(2109)^{-}$ are $m_{\Omega(2109)^{- =2108.5 \pm 5.2_{\rm stat}\,{\rm MeV /c^{2}$ and ${\Gamma _{\Omega(2109)^{- =18.3 \pm 16.4_{\rm stat}\,{\rm MeV .$ Systematic uncertainties on the measurements of the mass and width for the $\Omega(2109)^{-}$ mainly originate from the background shape, the mass shift, the resolution, the efficiency curve, the choice of sideband, and the parametrization of $\Omega(2012)^{-}$ and $\Omega(2109)^{-}$ signal shape.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-120: The impact of the background shape is evaluated by varying it to be a third-order Chebyshev polynomial.
elements.sent-2411-121: To consider the uncertainty from the parametrization of $\Omega(2012)^{-}$ signal shape, we change the fixed parameters of $\Omega(2012)^{-}$ adopted from PDG~\cite{pdg2024 by $\pm 1\sigma$, and the largest variation of the mass or width is considered as one contribution to this uncertainty; meanwhile, the variation of the mass and width when the $\Omega(2012)^{-}$ is alternatively fitted by the signal MC shape taking into account the shape distortion of $\Omega(2012)^{-}$ due to the finite phase space in $\Omega(2012)^{-}$ three-body decays (such as $\Omega(2012)^{- \to \Xi(1530) K \to \Xi \pi K$) is considered as another contribution to this uncertainty.}}
elements.sent-2411-122: The effect from the parametrization of $\Omega(2109)^{-}$ signal shape is studied by varying the double-Gaussian function convoluted with the Breit-Wigner to be a single-Gaussian function.
elements.sent-2411-123: The potential contributions from the feed-down components ($e^+e^- \to \Omega^- \bar{\Omega (2012/2109)^+, \bar{\Omega (2012/2109)^+ \to \bar{\Omega ^+ \pi \pi$) and the interference between $\Omega(2012)^{-}$ and $\Omega(2109)^{-}$ are investigated, while no evidence of these contributions is found at the current statistical level of the data.}}}
elements.sent-2411-124: The changes in mass and width when we introduce interference between $\Omega(2012)^{-}$ and $\Omega(2109)^{-}$ in the fit are quoted as systematic uncertainties.
elements.sent-2411-125: Following Refs.~\cite{LHCb:2017uwr, LHCb:2023sxp , the upper limit on the natural width of $\Omega(2109)^-$ is set at Bayesian 95\% confidence level, assuming Gaussian behavior for both statistical and systematic uncertainties, yielding ${\Gamma _{\Omega(2109)^{- <46.9\,{\rm MeV .$.}}}}}
elements.figure-2411-007: \setlength{\tabcolsep}{1mm} \caption{Absolute systematic uncertainties for the measurements of the resonance parameters of $\Omega(2109)^{-}$. } \renewcommand{\arraystretch}{1.0} \begin{tabular}{ccc} \specialrule{0em}{1pt}{1pt} \multirow{2}{*}{Source} Mass Width (${\rm MeV}/c^{2}$) ($\rm MeV$) Background shape 0.1 0.1 Mass shift 0.3 ··· Resolution ··· 4.7 Efficiency curve 0.1 0.6 Choice of sideband 0.1 0.2 Parametrization of $\Omega(2012)^{-}$ \multirow{2}{*}{0.1} \multirow{2}{*}{0.3} signal shape Parametrization of $\Omega(2109)^{-}$ \multirow{2}{*}{0.5} \multirow{2}{*}{2.4} signal shape Interference 0.6 2.0 Total 0.9 5.7 \end{tabular} \label{tab_sys}
elements.sent-2411-126: Following Ref.~\cite{zcs , we take the look-elsewhere effect and the relevant systematic uncertainties into account simultaneously to re-estimate the significance of the $\Omega(2109)^{-}$ using 5000 pseudodatasets to obtain the distribution of $\Delta({\rm 2ln}\mathcal{L )$.}}
elements.sent-2411-127: Combining with the minimum $\Delta({\rm 2ln}\mathcal{L )=23.6$ among all the systematic tests, the significance of the $\Omega(2109)^{-}$ is determined to be $n_{\sigma =4.1$ conservatively with $N_{\rm{dof = 3.27 + 0.03 = 3.30$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-128: For the significance of the $\Omega(2012)^{-}$, to account for the systematic effects, the minimum $\Delta({\rm 2ln}\mathcal{L )$ among all systematic variations is taken, which is still 12.2.}
elements.sent-2411-129: Combining with $N_{\rm{dof = 1$, we obtain $n_{\sigma =3.5$ for $\Omega(2012)^{-}$ including systematic uncertainties.}}}
elements.sent-2411-130: Since both of the significances of $\Omega(2012)^{-}$ and $\Omega(2109)^{-}$ are in the range of [3, 5]\,$\sigma$, we report the ratios of the average cross sections of $e^+ e^- \to \Omega(X)^{- \bar{\Omega ^{+ +c.c.$ to $e^+ e^- \to \Omega^{- \bar{\Omega ^{+}$, $R_{\Omega(X)^{- }$, in the range of $\sqrt{s \in [4.13, 4.70]\,GeV$ and also the corresponding upper limits at the 90\% confidence level, $R_{\Omega(X)^{- ^{\rm UL}$.}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-131: Here, $R_{\Omega(X)^{- \equiv \frac{\sigma(e^+ e^- \to \Omega(X)^{- \bar{\Omega ^{+ +c.c.) {\sigma(e^+ e^- \to \Omega^{- \bar{\Omega ^{+ )}$, with $\Omega(X)^{-}$ denoting $\Omega(2012)^{-}$ or $\Omega(2109)^{-}$, and are determined as \begin{equation}R_{\Omega(X)^{-}} \,=\, \frac{N_{\Omega(X)^{-}}}{N_{\Omega^{-}}} \left( \frac{(1+\delta^r)_{\Omega^{-}}}{(1+\delta^r)_{\Omega(X)^{-}}} \right) \left( \frac{\epsilon_{\Omega^{-}}}{\epsilon_{\Omega(X)^{-}}} \right) , \label{eq_cs_2109_all}\end{equation} where $N_{\Omega(X)^{- }$ and $N_{\Omega^{- }$ represent the numbers of events, $(1+\delta^r)_{\Omega(X)^{- }$ and $(1+\delta^r)_{\Omega^{- }$ represent the ISR correction factors, and $\epsilon_{\Omega(X)^{- }$ and $\epsilon_{\Omega^{- }$ represent the detection efficiencies for $e^+ e^- \to \Omega(X)^{- \bar{\Omega ^{+}$ and $e^+ e^- \to \Omega^{- \bar{\Omega ^{+}$, respectively.}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-132: Based on these values and Eq.~(\ref{eq_cs_2109_all ), we obtain $R_{\Omega(2012)^{- =0.36\pm0.13_{\rm stat}$ and $R_{\Omega(2109)^{- =0.93\pm0.35_{\rm stat}$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-133: The systematic uncertainties for $\frac{N_{\Omega(X)^{- {N_{\Omega^{- }$ come from sources similar to those for the measurements of the $\Omega(2109)^{-}$ parameters, including the background shape, the resolution, the efficiency curve, the choice of sideband, and the parametrization of $\Omega(2012)^{-}$ signal shape.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-134: Following the same method used in Ref.~\cite{BESIII:2024ext , the systematic uncertainties for $\frac{(1+\delta^r)_{\Omega^{- {(1+\delta^r)_{\Omega(X)^{- }$ are assigned by changing the input cross section line shape to the phase space line shape.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-135: The systematic uncertainties associated with $\frac{\epsilon_{\Omega^{- {\epsilon_{\Omega(X)^{- }$ are examined from two perspectives: (1) the differences in $\frac{\epsilon_{\Omega^{- {\epsilon_{\Omega(X)^{- }$ obtained from two different MC samples, with the recoil $\Omega(X)^{-}$, $\Omega^-$ decaying invisibly or exclusively to $\Xi K/\Lambda K$; (2) the variations in $\frac{\epsilon_{\Omega^{- {\epsilon_{\Omega(X)^{- }$ by changing the $\bar{\Omega ^{+}$ production angular model in the MC simulation to align with the expected distribution in a Generalized-Vector-Meson Dominance Model~\cite{Korner:1986vi , assuming the $J^P$ for $\Omega(2102)^-$ and $\Omega(2109)^-$ are $1/2^-$ and $3/2^-$, respectively.}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-136: Based on a Bayesian method~\cite{bayesian , the raw likelihood distributions versus the corresponding $R_{\Omega(X)^{- }$ are obtained.}}
elements.sent-2411-137: Based on this, $R_{\Omega(2012)^{- ^{\rm UL}$ and $R_{\Omega(2109)^{- ^{\rm UL}$ are set to be 0.57 and 1.31, respectively.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-138: In summary, using data samples with an integrated luminosity of 19\,fb$^{-1}$ collected by the BESIII detector at center-of-mass energies ranging from 4.13 to 4.70\,GeV, we find an evidence for the $\Omega(2109)^{-}$ hyperon through the process $e^+ e^- \to \Omega(2109)^{- \bar{\Omega ^{+}$ with a significance of 4.1\,$\sigma$ including both the look-elsewhere effect and systematic uncertainties.}}
elements.sent-2411-139: The upper limit on the natural width of $\Omega(2109)^-$ is set to be $46.9\,{\rm MeV}$ at 95\% confidence level.
elements.sent-2411-140: We also find evidence for $\Omega(2012)^{-}$ production via the process $e^+ e^- \to \Omega(2012)^{- \bar{\Omega ^{+}$, with a significance of 3.5\,$\sigma$ including systematic uncertainties.}}
elements.sent-2411-141: Furthermore, the ratios $R_{\Omega(X)^{- }$ of the average cross sections of $e^+ e^- \to \Omega(X)^{- \bar{\Omega ^{+ +c.c.$ and $e^+ e^- \to \Omega^{- \bar{\Omega ^{+}$ in the range of $\sqrt{s \in [4.13, 4.70]\,GeV$ are measured as $R_{\Omega(2012)^{- =0.36\pm0.13_{\rm stat}\pm0.04_{\rm syst}$ and $R_{\Omega(2109)^{- =0.93\pm0.35_{\rm stat}\pm0.13_{\rm syst}$, while the corresponding upper limits at the 90\% confidence level are set at $R_{\Omega(2012)^{- ^{\rm UL =0.57$ and $R_{\Omega(2109)^{- ^{\rm UL =1.31$.}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-142: Evidence for the $\Omega(2109)^{-}$ and the $\Omega(2012)^{-}$ reported in this Letter helps to fill in the missing excited states in the $\Omega^-$ hyperon spectrum.
elements.sent-2411-143: This consistency would imply that the $\Omega(2109)^{-}$ and $\Omega(2012)^{-}$ are probably conventional three-quark baryons and not molecules or other exotic states.
elements.sent-2411-144: The BEPCII upgrade, already implemented in 2024, will lead to much higher luminosity, allowing the confirmation of and parameter measurements for these two states to be investigated with larger data samples.
elements.sent-2411-145: Before 2018, the Particle Data Group (PDG)~\cite{pdg2018 only listed three $\Omega^{-}$ resonances beyond the ground state $\Omega^{-}$, i.e., the $\Omega(2250)^-$, $\Omega(2380)^-$, and $\Omega(2470)^-$, and none of these three are considered well-established.}
elements.sent-2411-146: The Skyrme model~\cite{Oh:2007cr , the quark model~\cite{Capstick:1986ter, Faustov:2015eba, Chao:1980em, Chen:2009de, Pervin:2007wa, Liu:2019wdr , the models with hyperfine interactions between quarks~\cite{An:2013zoa, An:2014lga , and lattice QCD~\cite{Engel:2013ig, Edwards:2012fx, Bulava:2010HSC,CLQCD:2015bgi all predict a $J^P = 1/2^-$ and $J^P = 3/2^-$ pair of $\Omega^-$ excitations with masses lying between 1.7 and 2.2 GeV$/c^{2}$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-147: A later study from the Belle Collaboration reported an updated value of the ratio, i.e. $0.99 \pm 0.26 \pm 0.06$, based on the same data sample, but with different event selection criteria and signal parametrization~\cite{Belle:2022mrg , which favors the molecular interpretation of the $\Omega(2012)^-$.}
elements.sent-2411-148: Simulated data samples produced with a {\sc Geant4 -based~\cite{geant4 Monte Carlo (MC) package, which includes the geometric description of the BESIII detector and the detector response, are used to determine detection efficiencies and to estimate backgrounds.}}
elements.sent-2411-149: The inclusive MC sample includes the production of open charm processes, the ISR production of vector charmonium(like) states, and the continuum processes incorporated in {\sc kkmc ~\cite{ref:kkmc .}}
elements.sent-2411-150: All particle decays are modeled with {\sc evtgen ~\cite{ref:evtgen using branching fractions either taken from the PDG~\cite{pdg2024 , when available, or otherwise estimated with {\sc lundcharm ~\cite{ref:lundcharm .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-151: Charged tracks detected in the multilayer drift chamber (MDC) are required to be within a polar angle ($\theta$) range of $|\rm{cos\theta |<0.93$, where $\theta$ is defined with respect to the $z$ axis, which is the symmetry axis of the MDC.}
elements.sent-2411-152: The $\bar{\Lambda}$ ($\bar{\Omega ^+$) candidates are reconstructed from $\bar{p}\pi^+$ ($\bar{\Lambda K^+$) pairs that are constrained to originate from a common vertex by requiring the $\chi^2$ of the vertex fits to be less than 50.}}
elements.sent-2411-153: A simultaneous unbinned maximum likelihood fit is performed to the $\bar{\Omega ^+$ recoil-mass spectra for the candidate events from the signal [Fig.~\ref{fig_recoil_mass_signal_region ] and sideband [Fig.~\ref{fig_recoil_mass_sideband_region ] regions.}}}
elements.sent-2411-154: In this fit, the signal shapes for the $\Omega(2012)^{-}$ and $\Omega(2109)^{-}$ are nonrelativistic Breit-Wigner functions convolved with a double-Gaussian resolution function; the $\Omega^{-}$ is described by an asymmetric double-Gaussian function, and the background is parametrized by an exponential function.
elements.sent-2411-155: The parameter of the exponential function is free and shared for both data samples from the signal and sideband regions.
elements.sent-2411-156: The statistical significances of the two $\Omega^{-}$ excitations are calculated by excluding the corresponding peak from the fit, finding the change in the log-likelihood $\Delta({\rm 2ln}\mathcal{L )$, and converting this to a $p$ value, taking into account the change in degrees of freedom ($N_{\rm{dof }$)~\cite{Wilks:1938dza .}}}
elements.sent-2411-157: The fitted mass and width of the $\Omega(2109)^{-}$ are $m_{\Omega(2109)^{- =2108.5 \pm 5.2_{\rm stat}\,{\rm MeV /c^{2}$ and ${\Gamma _{\Omega(2109)^{- =18.3 \pm 16.4_{\rm stat}\,{\rm MeV .$ Systematic uncertainties on the measurements of the mass and width for the $\Omega(2109)^{-}$ mainly originate from the background shape, the mass shift, the resolution, the efficiency curve, the choice of sideband, and the parametrization of the $\Omega(2012)^{-}$ and $\Omega(2109)^{-}$ signal shape.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-158: The systematic uncertainty related to the choice of sideband is estimated by ten different variations in the edges of the sideband intervals, by amounts ranging from 5 to 20\,MeV/$c^2$, and the maximum change of the mass or width compared to the nominal one is taken as the uncertainty.
elements.sent-2411-159: To consider the uncertainty from the parametrization of the $\Omega(2012)^{-}$ signal shape, we change the fixed parameters of $\Omega(2012)^{-}$ adopted from PDG~\cite{pdg2024 by $\pm 1\sigma$, and the largest variation of the mass or width is considered as one contribution to this uncertainty; meanwhile, the variation of the mass and width when the $\Omega(2012)^{-}$ is alternatively fitted by the signal MC shape taking into account the shape distortion of $\Omega(2012)^{-}$ due to the finite phase space in $\Omega(2012)^{-}$ three-body decays (such as $\Omega(2012)^{- \to \Xi(1530) K \to \Xi \pi K$) is considered as another contribution to this uncertainty.}}
elements.sent-2411-160: Following Ref.~\cite{zcs , we take the look-elsewhere effect and the relevant systematic uncertainties into account simultaneously to reestimate the significance of the $\Omega(2109)^{-}$ using 5000 pseudodatasets to obtain the distribution of $\Delta({\rm 2ln}\mathcal{L )$.}}

elements.sent-2411-164: Theoretical models~\cite{Oh:2007cr, Capstick:1986ter, Faustov:2015eba, Chao:1980em, Chen:2009de, Pervin:2007wa, Liu:2019wdr, An:2013zoa, An:2014lga, Engel:2013ig, Edwards:2012fx, Bulava:2010HSC,CLQCD:2015bgi predict a $J^P = 1/2^-$ and $J^P = 3/2^-$ pair of $\Omega^-$ excitations within the mass region from 1.7 to 2.2 GeV$/c^{2}$, but with large variations in their mass predictions.}
